data.title: German drone startup Quantum Systems secures $1.2B at $8B valuation
data.description:
Quantum Systems has raised $1.2B in a new funding round, more than doubling the German drone maker's valuation to about $8B post-money.

The round was co-led by Blackstone (BX), Noteus, Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY), and Advent. The company said that the proceeds will support international expansion, boost manufacturing capacity, and fund acquisitions.

Based in Gilching, Bavaria, Quantum Systems is among several German defense startups capitalizing on Europe's surge in military spending. The company, which develops unmanned aerial vehicles, raised capital in November at a valuation of €3B. It has supplied surveillance drones to Ukraine since Russia's full-scale invasion and has since expanded its portfolio to include unmanned land and maritime systems.

Peter Thiel is one of Quantum Systems' early investors. The billionaire entrepreneur, a co-founder of Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and a prominent supporter of U.S. President Donald Trump, has drawn attention in Germany over his investments in defense startups.

However, the company said it has not faced scrutiny from the German government over Thiel's investment. Earlier this year, Germany's Defense Ministry briefed lawmakers on Thiel's minority stake in Stark Defence, according to Bloomberg.

data.title: Google loses appeal over $4.7B EU Android antitrust fine
data.description:
• Alphabet’s Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) lost its long-running battle against a €4.1B ($4.7B) European Union antitrust fine after the bloc's top court upheld regulators' findings that the U.S. tech giant abused the market dominance of its Android operating system.
  • The European Court of Justice ruled on Thursday that Google's appeal against the European Commission's penalty should be dismissed. The decision is legally binding, marking a major victory for the Brussels-based regulator in a case that began when the fine was imposed in 2018.
  • “The appeal brought by Google and its parent company Alphabet against the judgment of the General Court is dismissed, thereby confirming the penalty imposed for Google Search’s abuse of a dominant position in the context of the Android operating system,” the court said in a statement on the judgment.

data.title: Taiwan detains two Super Micro employees in alleged Nvidia AI server export probe - report
data.description:
Taiwanese prosecutors detained two Super Micro (SMCI) employees, while two others were released on bail and barred from leaving Taiwan over the alleged illegal export of advanced AI servers containing Nvidia (NVDA) chips to China, Reuters reported.

In a letter to U.S. customers, Chief Revenue Officer Matthew Thauberger said the four employees were questioned on June 29 in connection with an investigation into the company's sales to a technology company in Taiwan.

"Two of the four employees have been detained pending a hearing, and the other two have been released on bail," Thauberger wrote in the letter.

"Super Micro is not a target of this investigation," he said, adding that the company has been cooperating with Taiwanese authorities for several months and has provided investigators access to the employees' desks and electronic devices and placed all four on administrative leave pending the outcome of the probe.

Super Micro (SMCI) distributor Albatron Technology has also been detained. An employee of data center operator Chief Telecom was also questioned earlier this week.

Those detained this week are in addition to three people taken into custody in May in the ongoing investigation.

The three detained in May are suspected of falsifying export documents to smuggle Super Micro (SMCI) servers containing Nvidia (NVDA) AI chips to China. Prosecutors allege they successfully shipped at least one batch of chips and attempted to export about 50 servers that were seized before leaving Taiwan.

data.title: Volkswagen braces for turbulent talks ahead of critical board meeting over massive restructuring
Coca-Cola eyes $1B IPO for India bottling arm at nearly $10B valuation
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) is preparing to list its Indian bottling arm, Hindustan Coca-Cola Beverages ((HCCB)), in an IPO in 2027 that could raise about $1B at a valuation of nearly $10B, multiple media houses reported.

The IPO could take place as early as next year, although the timing, size, and structure of the offering remain under discussion and could change.

The company laid the groundwork for the listing last year by selling a 40% stake in HCCB's parent entity to the Jubilant Bhartia Group.

HCCB operates 15 manufacturing plants across India and reported FY25 revenue of ₹12,751 crore.

Kotak, HDFC Group, and Citibank are advising on the planned listing, which could become one of India's largest consumer IPOs of 2026 if markets remain favorable.

Coca-Cola has not publicly confirmed the IPO plans, while discussions with advisers are ongoin

data.title: Samsung details $90B South Korea investment across chips, displays, batteries
Asian markets mostly lower as Wall Street tech selloff weighs on sentiment
Amazon developing custom on-device AI chips for Echo and Fire TV lineups: report
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) is focusing on building chips for its “critical” consumer devices, the company’s top hardware executive told CNBC.

“We do make our own end-to-end silicon for the devices that we ship,” Panos Panay, the head of devices and services at Amazon, said. He said Amazon’s custom silicon is in devices such as the Echo Show 8, Echo Show 11, and Fire TV.

In October, Amazon unveiled the AZ3 and AZ3 Pro chips designed to run AI models on-device rather than in the cloud.

“On some of the more critical devices right now, our focus is end-to-end silicon, because to your point, if you really want that hardware and software connection ... and if we’re going to go deliver this ambient experience in the home for people in the most secure way, we definitely need to think about how that end-to-end delivery of hardware comes together,” Panay said.

He added that the company still also uses chips from companies like Qualcomm (QCOM).

Asked what kind of gadgets the company was working on, Panay said: “When you think about the future of AI devices, you got to be super skeptical right now for anyone who tells you they know what they are. I have a lab full of devices.”

Panay said there is a “whole roadmap of on-the-go devices.” The executive described these devices as gadgets that people carry with them, that collect data and that people talk to.

data.title: Civeo prices $100M convertible notes, wins six-year Canada contract renewal
data.description:
• Civeo (CVEO) priced a private offering of $100M of 4.50% convertible senior notes due 2031, and announced a six-year contract renewal for workforce accommodation and hospitality services in Western Canada.
  • The convertible notes, offered to qualified institutional buyers, will pay 4.50% annual interest, maturing on Aug. 1, 2031, unless earlier repurchased, redeemed, or converted. The initial conversion rate is 24.6840 shares per $1,000 principal amount, equivalent to a conversion price of about $40.51 per share, representing a 20% premium to Civeo's July 1 closing price of $33.76.
  • The notes are expected to close on July 7. Initial purchasers have a 13-day option to buy up to an additional $15M of notes.
  • Separately, one of Civeo's Western Canada joint ventures secured a six-year contract renewal to continue providing workforce accommodations and hospitality services.
  • The renewed agreement extends through June 30, 2032, replacing existing arrangements that were due to expire in 2027.

data.title: EU chip industry squeezed by China, U.S. dependence
data.description:
The European Union's semiconductor sector is under increasing pressure from Chinese export controls, dependence on U.S. technology, and structural weaknesses in its domestic chip industry, according to an EU-funded report.

"From the perspective of European semiconductor firms and RTOs, the future of technology transfer regimes looks bleak," the report by the European Union Institute for Security Studies and Institut Montaigne said.

The report said the EU relies heavily on the U.S. for critical semiconductor technologies, including electronic design automation software and advanced chip technologies, while China remains a key market and supplier of critical raw materials such as rare earths and magnets. It warned that escalating U.S.-China technology tensions leave European firms vulnerable to policy decisions beyond their control.

"While Beijing still appears to be the biggest threat, dependence on Washington seems to have become of much greater concern under the second Trump administration," co-author Joris Teer told Reuters.

The report also cited persistently high energy prices, limited private capital investment and the decline of Europe's chip-consuming industries as factors undermining the sector's competitiveness.

It pointed that Europe is unlikely to achieve full semiconductor self-sufficiency and should instead pursue "strategic interdependence" by reducing excessive dependencies while deepening cooperation with trusted partners. The report recommended building on existing strengths, particularly ASML's leadership in chipmaking equipment, investing across the semiconductor value chain and preparing for a more fragmented global technology landscape.

The European Commission's proposed Chips Act 2.0 aims to boost demand for EU-made chips and strengthen allied semiconductor supply chains.
